deductions.reason: In Transcript 1, Turn 10, the tutor provides a direct two-sentence summary contrasting act consequentialism with the student's view in near-submission-ready language ('Act consequentialism says the right act is whichever produces the best overall results, so saving five at the cost of one will often look justified. Your view says outcomes are not the only thing that matter, because it can be wrong to intentionally use one person as the means of saving the five, even if more lives are saved.'). This does the final bridging reasoning work for the student rather than prompting the student to articulate it themselves.

Across all three transcripts, when students successfully articulate their core principle (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 9, Transcript 2 Turn 9, Transcript 3 Turn 10), the tutor moves forward with next steps but does not prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why their improved formulation worked better than earlier attempts.

grade.overview: The tutor generally maintained a Socratic approach across all three transcripts, consistently prompting students to articulate their own views and test them against cases. The main deduction is in 1.1 for Transcript 1 Turn 10, where the tutor provided a polished two-sentence summary that effectively did the final reasoning work for the student, crossing into near-submission-ready territory. The 1.1.C.b deduction (6 points) is applied once for the bundle. A smaller deduction is applied for 1.3.C.a across all transcripts, as the tutor consistently missed opportunities to prompt reflection on what changed when students improved. No deductions were warranted for scaffolding, dialogue quality, or communication quality.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is 1.1.C.b (6 points): in Transcript 1 Turn 10, the tutor directly stated both the act consequentialist position and the student's contrasting view in polished, submission-ready language after the student asked for a summary, doing the final synthesis work rather than guiding the student to produce it. This is the clearest violation across the bundle. The 1.3.C.a deduction (2 points) applies because in all three transcripts, when students successfully reached their core principle, the tutor moved to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking. No 1.1.A.a deduction is warranted because the tutor did not provide a full submission-ready answer across turns; the Turn 10 summary in Transcript 1 is a single isolated instance of giving the student's view back to them, not a gradual assembly of a complete answer. Transcripts 2 and 3 maintained strong Socratic discipline throughout, with the tutor consistently asking students to generate and test their own principles.
